metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between the `keep=RETAIN_PER_TICKET` default-parameter approach in Scope and the monkeypatch-at-runtime requirement in AC2(c): either (a) specify that `prune` reads `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` from the module inside the function body (not as a captured default) so monkeypatching works, or (b) rewrite AC2(c) to use `importlib.reload` or direct `keep=` override instead of a bare monkeypatch.
evidence: Scope: 'add a `prune(ticket_id, *, keep=RETAIN_PER_TICKET, repo_root=None)` function'
AC2: '(c) monkeypatches the single constant to a small N and asserts BOTH prune paths then retain N (proving both paths read the one constant at prune time, not a captured copy)'
In Python, `def f(x=SOME_CONST)` captures the value of SOME_CONST at definition time; patching the module attribute afterward leaves the default unchanged.
findings.finding: The Scope section specifies `prune(..., keep=RETAIN_PER_TICKET)` as a Python default parameter, which binds the value at function-definition time. AC2(c) then requires a monkeypatch of the single constant to make BOTH prune paths retain N — but monkeypatching a module-level name after import does NOT retroactively change an already-bound default parameter in Python, so the monkeypatch test described in AC2 would fail with the implementation approach described in Scope.
findings.location: Scope (prune signature) vs. Acceptance Criteria AC2(c)
scenarios: An implementer follows the Scope spec literally (default param) and writes the AC2(c) monkeypatch test; the test fails because the default was already bound at import time, sending the implementer in two directions — either change the implementation or weaken the test.
findings.suggested_fix: In Scope, change the prune signature to `prune(ticket_id, *, keep=None, repo_root=None)` and resolve `keep` inside the body as `keep = keep if keep is not None else plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET`. This way the constant is read at call time, the default-parameter object-identity test (AC2a/b) still holds for the `None` sentinel, and the monkeypatch test (AC2c) works correctly. Alternatively, rewrite AC2(c) to patch via a module reload.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace or supplement the AC2 `is`-identity assertion with a check that `code_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` is literally the same object as imported from `plan_review.sidecar` in a way that survives integer interning — e.g. assert `vars(code_review.sidecar)['RETAIN_PER_TICKET'] is vars(plan_review.sidecar)['RETAIN_PER_TICKET']` after reloading both modules, or rely solely on the monkeypatch sub-test (AC2c) which correctly distinguishes an import from a copy.
evidence: CPython interns integers in the range [-5, 256]; `int(50) is int(50)` is True even for independently-defined literals in different modules.
The plan explicitly claims the `is` check means 'a copied literal that merely equals 50 fails' — this claim is factually incorrect for the value 50.
plan_review/sidecar.py:43: RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 10 (will become 50) — both values fall within CPython's interning range.
findings.finding: The AC2 oracle uses Python `is` (object identity) to verify that code_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET is the imported constant rather than a copied literal, but CPython interns all small integers (−5 to 256), so a copied `RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50` in code_review/sidecar.py would also satisfy `code_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET is plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` — the oracle cannot distinguish an import from a copied literal at this value, defeating its stated purpose.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 oracle: 'asserts `code_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET is plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` (OBJECT IDENTITY, so a copied literal that merely equals 50 fails)'
scenarios: An implementer adds `RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50` as a second independent literal in code_review/sidecar.py instead of importing it; the AC2 `is`-identity assertion passes anyway due to integer interning, giving a false green.

The monkeypatch sub-test in AC2(c) would still catch a copied literal (since patching plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET won't affect a copied local), so the oracle is partially salvageable — but the primary `is` assertion cannot serve as its stated discriminator.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the `is` sub-assertion from AC2 (it cannot distinguish import from copied literal for values ≤ 256) and rely on AC2(c)'s monkeypatch test as the sole oracle for the single-constant invariant: monkeypatching `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` to a small N and asserting both prune paths then retain N proves both paths read the one constant at call time, which a copied literal would fail.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between Scope's default-parameter design and AC2(c)'s monkeypatch oracle: either (a) change the prune signature to use a `None` sentinel resolved at call time (`keep=None` → `keep = RETAIN_PER_TICKET if keep is None else keep`), so the module-level name is read on every call and the monkeypatch works, or (b) rewrite AC2(c) to pass `keep=N` explicitly and test the constant-identity assertion separately via `__defaults__` inspection.
evidence: Scope: 'add a `prune(ticket_id, *, keep=RETAIN_PER_TICKET, repo_root=None)` function'
AC2(c): 'monkeypatches the single constant to a small N and asserts BOTH prune paths then retain N (proving both paths read the one constant at prune time, not a captured copy)'
Python semantics: default parameter values are bound at function definition time, not at call time — patching the module attribute after import does not change the bound default
Confirmed in existing code: plan_review/sidecar.py line 67 `def prune(ticket_id: str, *, keep: int = RETAIN_PER_TICKET, ...)` — same pattern, same issue
findings.finding: The plan mandates `prune(..., keep=RETAIN_PER_TICKET)` as a Python default parameter (Scope section) while simultaneously requiring AC2(c) to monkeypatch the single module-level constant and assert both prune paths honor the patched value. Python binds default argument values at function-definition time, so patching `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` AFTER the function is defined does not change the already-captured default. AC2(c) as written is mechanically unachievable with the default-parameter design AC2(b) and Scope mandate — the monkeypatch test will always see the original bound value (50), not the patched small N.
findings.location: Scope section (`prune(..., keep=RETAIN_PER_TICKET, ...)`) and AC2(c) (`monkeypatches the single constant … asserts BOTH prune paths then retain N`)
scenarios: A test does `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 3`, then calls `code_review.sidecar.prune(ticket_id)` with no explicit `keep` — prune retains 50 (the bound default), not 3, so the assertion fails.
AC2(c) is written specifically to catch 'a captured copy' but the default-parameter pattern IS a captured copy — the test is unpassable as designed.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the prune signature to `def prune(ticket_id, *, keep=None, repo_root=None)` and resolve `keep = RETAIN_PER_TICKET if keep is None else keep` inside the function body. This makes the module-level constant read at call time, so AC2(c)'s monkeypatch works correctly. Update AC2(b) to check `prune.__defaults__` or the sentinel pattern accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile AC2's object-identity sub-clause (b) with its monkeypatch sub-clause (c): a `from X import NAME` + default-parameter pattern satisfies (b) at load time via CPython int interning but cannot satisfy (c)'s late-binding semantic — specify a concrete implementation that satisfies both (e.g. `keep=None` sentinel with `if keep is None: keep = plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` in the body, updating the AC2(b) oracle to check `code_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET is plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` at module level rather than the function default).
evidence: Scope says: 'reference the shared bound (import `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` from `rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar`)' and 'add a `prune(ticket_id, *, keep=RETAIN_PER_TICKET, ...)` function'
AC2(c) says: 'monkeypatches the single constant to a small N and asserts BOTH prune paths then retain N (proving both paths read the one constant at prune time, not a captured copy)'

Python semantics: `from module import NAME` binds the current value of NAME; `def f(x=NAME)` captures that integer object at definition time — a subsequent `module.NAME = N` does not update f's default

plan_review.sidecar.prune line 67: `def prune(ticket_id: str, *, keep: int = RETAIN_PER_TICKET, ...)` — same pattern, so plan_review's prune default also cannot be monkeypatched via the module attribute after load
findings.finding: AC2's monkeypatch oracle (sub-clause c) is structurally incompatible with the import strategy specified in Scope. The plan says `from rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar import RETAIN_PER_TICKET` and then `def prune(..., keep: int = RETAIN_PER_TICKET, ...)`. In Python, a default-parameter value is captured at function-definition time as the integer object then bound to that name — not re-read from `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` at call time. After monkeypatching `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET = N`, `code_review.sidecar.prune`'s default `keep` is still the old integer object, so AC2(c)'s assertion that 'both prune paths then retain N' will fail. The import + default-parameter pattern cannot satisfy the late-binding semantic the test oracle requires.
findings.location: Scope section (code_review.sidecar — import + prune signature) and AC2 (sub-clauses b and c)
scenarios: An executor implementing exactly what Scope says (import + default param) writes the AC2(c) monkeypatch test; it fails because prune's default keep is the pre-patch integer object, not the new N

The executor works around it by reading `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` lazily inside the function body, but then AC2(b)'s 'default parameter IS that same object' assertion fails — the default would be a sentinel like None
The executor duplicates the literal 50 in a local `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` to pass AC2(b)'s `is` check, but that creates the 'second literal' the plan explicitly forbids, and AC2(c) still fails
findings.suggested_fix: In code_review.sidecar, use a module-level re-export: `from rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar import RETAIN_PER_TICKET` (satisfies the `is` check at module level for AC2a). Define prune with a `None` sentinel default: `def prune(ticket_id, *, keep=None, repo_root=None): if keep is None: from rebar.llm.plan_review import sidecar as _pr; keep = _pr.RETAIN_PER_TICKET`. Update AC2(b) to assert the MODULE attribute identity (`code_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET is plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET`) rather than the function default, and AC2(c) works because the monkeypatched value is read lazily at call time.

findings.finding: AC2's stated oracle ('a test asserting both prune paths honor the same value') is weaker than AC2's stated criterion ('The cap is one named constant governing both paths'). A mirrored-constants implementation (two separate variables both set to 50) satisfies the oracle but violates the 'one named constant' structural claim, meaning the AC can be green while the criterion is false.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'The cap is one named constant governing both plan- and code-review prune paths. Oracle: a test asserting both prune paths honor the same value.'
findings.suggested_fix: AC2 oracle: 'a test asserting `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET is code_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` (import identity), confirming a single definition site; plus `grep -c "RETAIN_PER_TICKET\s*=" src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` should equal 1 (only one assignment exists in the codebase).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise AC2(c) to specify an oracle that is achievable under Python's default-parameter semantics: either (a) require the implementation to resolve `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` at call-time (e.g., `keep=None` sentinel with `if keep is None: keep = RETAIN_PER_TICKET` in the body) so monkeypatching the module attribute does take effect, and update the Scope section accordingly; or (b) replace the monkeypatch test with a direct behavioral assertion — e.g., temporarily call `prune(keep=5)` on both paths and assert 5 are retained — and separately assert object identity for the default, making the two claims explicit and achievable.
evidence: AC2(c) text: "monkeypatches the single constant to a small N and asserts BOTH prune paths then retain N (proving both paths read the one constant at prune time, not a captured copy)"

Python semantics: `def prune(..., keep=RETAIN_PER_TICKET)` captures the integer value 50 at definition time; re-assigning `module.RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 5` after import does not alter the already-captured default
Scope section specifies `prune(ticket_id, *, keep=RETAIN_PER_TICKET, repo_root=None)` — a standard default-argument form that will not respond to post-import monkeypatching
findings.finding: AC2(c)'s monkeypatch oracle is non-functional as specified: Python captures the default-parameter value at function-definition time, so monkeypatching `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` after import does NOT change the `keep` default of either `prune()` function. A test calling `prune()` with no args after monkeypatching will still use the old value (50), causing the test to assert the wrong thing or fail silently as a no-op validator — a self-authored-oracle risk that locks in the impression of live-constant-reading without actually proving it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2(c): "monkeypatches the single constant to a small N and asserts BOTH prune paths then retain N"
scenarios: Developer writes the monkeypatch test as described; calls `prune()` with no explicit `keep`; the default is still 50; test either fails or the developer patches it to pass by calling `prune(keep=monkeypatched_value)` explicitly — which no longer proves the default reads the live constant
Test suite goes green while the oracle proves nothing about live-constant-reading, locking in a false assurance
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, change `prune(ticket_id, *, keep=RETAIN_PER_TICKET, repo_root=None)` to use a `None` sentinel: `prune(ticket_id, *, keep=None, repo_root=None)` with `if keep is None: keep = RETAIN_PER_TICKET` inside the body. This makes the constant read at call time, so a monkeypatch test (set `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 5`, call both `prune()` with no args, assert 5 retained) will actually work as intended. Update AC2 to match.

findings.finding: AC2's oracle ('a test asserting both prune paths honor the same value') only verifies value-equality (both == 50), not structural sharing. It would pass even with mirrored constants, which contradicts AC2's own text ('The cap is one named constant governing both paths').
findings.suggested_fix: Update AC2 oracle to: 'a test importing both sidecar modules and asserting `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET is code_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` (object identity), or asserting both reference the same fully-qualified constant from a shared module.'

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit DATA LOSS RISK note to the Compatibility & Rollback section stating that code-review REVIEW_RESULT events git-rm'd on first emit are irrecoverable by source-code revert alone, and assert the post-rollback data state (e.g., 'events already pruned remain deleted; only events retained at prune time are present after rollback').
evidence: Plan quote: 'removes the code-review prune (code-review growth becomes unbounded again); no migration'
Absence: no assertion that code-review events pruned during the live window are irrecoverable after rollback

T11 POST-ROLLBACK SCHEMA STATE rule: 'the plan must assert on the schema STATE AFTER rollback … Where destructiveness is ambiguous, default to compensating-forward and attach a literal DATA LOSS RISK note'
findings.finding: The rollback section states the post-rollback behavioural outcome ('code-review growth becomes unbounded again; no migration') but never asserts the post-rollback DATA STATE: code-review sidecar events that were git-rm'd and committed on first emit after deployment are permanently deleted and unrecoverable by reverting the code change alone (only git-log archaeology can surface them). T11 requires the plan to assert the schema/data STATE after rollback, not merely that rollback exits cleanly, and where destructiveness is ambiguous a literal DATA LOSS RISK note is required.
scenarios: Operator deploys the change; first code-review emit fires and git-rm's events 51–N (potentially dozens of rounds of history); operator then decides to revert due to a bug; the revert removes the prune logic but the pruned events are gone from the tickets branch — they cannot be recovered by reverting source code alone
findings.suggested_fix: In the Compatibility & Rollback section, append: 'DATA LOSS RISK: Code-review REVIEW_RESULT events deleted by the first post-deploy prune are permanently removed from the tickets branch; reverting the source change restores the prune-suppression logic but does NOT restore deleted events. Post-rollback data state: only the ≤50 events present at first-emit time survive; older events are gone. Recovery requires replaying git-log on the tickets branch to identify rm'd blobs, which is operationally expensive — operators should snapshot the tickets branch HEAD before deploying if full history must be preserved.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Differentiate AC1 and AC3 or merge them: if AC3 is meant to specifically verify that `emit()` triggers pruning (not merely that the count is right), reword AC3 to make that structural distinction explicit (e.g. 'the prune is invoked from within emit, not only from an external caller'); otherwise merge AC3 into AC1 to eliminate the near-duplicate.
evidence: AC1: 'a unit test appending N>50 synthetic events on each path and asserting the retained count is 50 and the newest are kept' — 'each path' includes code-review
AC3: 'a test that emitting past the cap on a code-review target ticket leaves exactly 50 events'
findings.finding: AC1 and AC3 are near-duplicate for the code-review path: AC1 already covers 'BOTH the plan-review and the code-review sidecar' with a test appending N>50 events and asserting retained count is 50; AC3 then re-covers exactly the same behavior ('emitting past the cap on a code-review target ticket leaves exactly 50 events') as a separate criterion.
scenarios: Implementer writes one test covering both; a reviewer flags AC3 as unsatisfied; or implementer writes duplicate tests for no additional coverage.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how concurrent prune() calls on the same ticket are handled — either document that the exception-swallowing contract is sufficient (lost prune → corrected on next emit, off-by-one is acceptable), or add a file-lock/git-lock around the list+rm+commit sequence.
evidence: 'list the ticket's -REVIEW_RESULT.json events by filename timestamp, git rm all but the newest keep, commit with --no-verify'
No mention of a file lock, git lock, or serialization mechanism for concurrent prune calls
T9 checklist: 'Shared/mutable state mutated atomically (lock/CAS/transaction, no check-then-act TOCTOU)'
Exception-swallowing means one of the concurrent prune attempts silently fails, but the surviving prune may have operated on a stale file list
findings.finding: The list-then-delete sequence inside prune() is a TOCTOU race: two concurrent emit() calls on the same ticket both snapshot the file list, both decide which files to delete, and then both attempt git rm + commit on an overlapping set — one will operate on already-deleted files or face a conflicting commit, with no coordination mechanism specified.
findings.location: Scope section — code_review/sidecar.py prune spec; plan_review/sidecar.py prune (mirrored design)
scenarios: Two drift-refresh reviews complete nearly simultaneously on a ticket with 51 events; both prune() calls list 51 files, both attempt to git rm the same oldest file, second git rm fails silently, commit races — retained count may be 49 or 51 instead of 50.
High-concurrency CI environment runs multiple code-review gates in parallel on the same ticket; prune() races leave the branch in an inconsistent state that only self-corrects on the next emit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least a `logger.warning(...)` (or equivalent structured log) inside the exception-swallowing block of both `prune()` functions so that failures are observable in logs, even if they don't propagate.
evidence: 'best-effort and exception-swallowing — a failed prune never fails the gate'
No mention of logging, metrics, or alerting on prune failure anywhere in the plan
Observability sub-criterion: 'New failure points instrumented with a metric/log/trace/alert'
findings.finding: The prune path explicitly swallows all exceptions with no logging or metric emission, so if pruning fails systematically (e.g., a git permissions regression, index lock contention, or misconfigured repo_root), events accumulate unboundedly — recreating the original problem — with zero operator visibility.
findings.location: Scope section — code_review/sidecar.py prune spec: 'best-effort and exception-swallowing — a failed prune never fails the gate'; plan_review/sidecar.py prune (same design by implication)
scenarios: A git index lock left by a crashed process causes every prune() to raise; all REVIEW_RESULT events accumulate silently; auditors lose history and the tickets branch grows unboundedly; no alert fires.
A permission regression on the tickets branch silently disables pruning for all tickets; operators discover it only when disk/performance is impacted.
findings.suggested_fix: In the except block of each prune(), add: `logger.warning('sidecar prune failed for ticket %s: %s', ticket_id, exc, exc_info=True)`. This satisfies the observability requirement without changing the best-effort contract.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
